findings.description: Literal acceptance criteria satisfied: TestCrashInjectionRecovery truncates both a torn-payload and a torn-header tail on the last segment, asserts Replay returns nil error, applies all prior valid records exactly once in order, and discards the torn bytes. Test name and -run pattern match the issue's test spec exactly. The torn-vs-corrupt distinction (torn = incomplete bytes at tail, silently discarded; corrupt = full-length record with bad CRC, hard error) is applied consistently in three places sharing one code path (parseSegmentRecords): OpenWriter's resume path (repairTornTail), ReadSegment, and readSegmentFrom/Replay. Verified by reading writer.go/recovery.go and their diffs — no place applies discard-on-CRC-mismatch or hard-errors-on-genuine-torn-tail, so the two failure modes are not conflated in either direction.

docs/LLD/wal.md is stale scaffold-only prose (no reference to segment record header format, torn-tail/CRC distinction, fsync-durability semantics, Offset()/checkpoint pairing, or per-segment torn-tail-only-on-last-segment rule). This predates 1.3.5 and was already flagged as an open LLD-sync gap in prior verification runs; 1.3.5 does not update it. Not a code defect, but the LLD continues to drift further from the as-built invariants (e.g. it says nothing about the non-last-segment torn-tail hard-error policy this subtask newly introduces). Recommend a follow-up LLD sync subtask before closing out the WAL crash-safety epic, since new consumers (e.g. the future Checkpoint caller referenced in Offset()'s doc comment) will rely on documented invariants, not just code comments.

Confirmed via `git diff --stat` and `git diff -- engine/wal/writer_test.go engine/wal/recovery_test.go` that all changes to the two pre-existing test files are purely additive (no deleted/modified lines), and full `go test ./...` at the engine module root passes together with all previously-listed regression-guard tests (TestSegmentWriter, TestAppendOversizedRecordHardErrors, TestOpenWriterResumesExistingSegments, record_test.go and checkpoint_test.go suites, TestRecoveryReplay*, TestRecoveryReplayInvalidRecordType) unmodified and green. The recovery.go refactor (readSegmentFrom now returns tornTail bool and records-before-error) changes Replay's internal control flow but is behavior-preserving for all pre-existing passing scenarios: the new readErr-after-apply-loop ordering only changes behavior in the previously-buggy case (CRC corruption after valid records), which is exactly the bug fix claimed and is now covered by TestReplayCRCCorruption.

Tested/verified: empty segment (parseSegmentRecords loop never executes when data is empty -> tornTail=false, no false positive on freshly rotated empty last segment); torn header vs torn payload (both covered by dedicated subtests); torn tail exactly at record boundary (off == len(data) exits loop cleanly, tornTail=false, matches readSegmentFrom's documented contract for a checkpoint offset landing exactly at segment end); multi-segment scenario for the non-last-segment hard-error case (TestCrashInjectionRecoveryTornTailInNonLastSegmentErrors forces rotation across many small segments then truncates a non-last one).

No new I/O paths, no secrets/credentials handled; crash_subprocess_test.go only re-execs the test binary itself with an internal env-var flag scoped to the test process tree and reads back plain WAL record bytes it wrote itself. No plaintext credential exposure introduced.

Offset() takes w.mu identically to SegmentNum(), so it's correctly serialized against Append/rotateLocked. `go test -race` is clean across the whole package, run 5x with the subprocess-crash test targeted, no flakes. Minor coverage gap: no test exercises Offset() called concurrently from another goroutine while Append is in-flight (a race would only be caught if such a test existed) — low severity since the locking pattern is a direct, trivial mirror of the pre-existing SegmentNum().

TestCrashInjectionRecoveryTornTailInNonLastSegmentErrors's stricter-than-literal behavior (hard error on torn tail in a non-last segment) does not contradict the issue's literal wording, which only specifies behavior for 'the' torn record produced by a crash (necessarily in the actively-written, i.e. last, segment); the new test covers a scenario the issue is silent on and the added policy is a defensible closed-world invariant (only the last segment can legitimately be torn), not a weakening of the required discard-and-proceed behavior. No test exists for a torn tail in a non-last segment when there is only one segment total (i.e., last-segment-only WAL) combined with a checkpoint pointer referencing an already-archived, deleted segment — an edge case outside this subtask's scope but worth flagging as a coverage gap for whichever subtask implements checkpoint archival/truncation.

parseSegmentRecords is now the single shared implementation behind OpenWriter's repairTornTail, ReadSegment, and readSegmentFrom, eliminating the previous duplicated hand-rolled parsing loop in readSegmentFrom (confirmed via diff: the old ~30-line manual loop in recovery.go was deleted and replaced with a call into writer.go's shared helper). This reduces the risk of the torn/corrupt semantics drifting apart between the writer-resume path and the replay path, which is exactly the previously-deferred gap (a) this subtask targets.

test_run_summary.result: all green, no flakes across 5 repeated runs of the subprocess-crash and crash-injection tests
